source.title: Psychophysical methods, or how to measure a threshold, and why

study.title: Psychophysical methods, or how to measure a threshold, and why
study.population: Psychophysics methods reference rather than a participant cohort.
study.design: Methodological chapter describing threshold estimation with controlled repeated stimuli.
study.notes: Use as task-design and interpretation context for Boundary orientation discrimination, not as a reference distribution.

samples.label: Psychophysics threshold methods reference
samples.population: Methodological literature rather than measured Boundary-comparable participants.
samples.inclusionCriteria: Chapter-level methodological discussion of psychophysical threshold estimation.
samples.exclusionCriteria: No participant-level orientation-discrimination distribution is extracted.
samples.notes: This sample placeholder lets the literature schema cite a reviewed methods result without implying cohort comparability.

tasks.label: Orientation discrimination threshold measurement
tasks.taskVariant: Repeated visual discrimination at controlled stimulus levels with threshold estimation.
tasks.notes: Boundary currently uses a short web task and coarse smallest-tilt summary, not a fitted psychometric threshold.

measures.label: Estimated orientation threshold
measures.transformation: Methodological extraction only; Boundary reports the smallest tested tilt reaching the criterion rather than fitting a psychometric function.
measures.notes: Reviewed for construct context about how to interpret threshold-like orientation summaries.

statisticJson.takeaway: Visual thresholds are most defensible when estimated from repeated task performance at controlled stimulus levels.

results.notes: Descriptive methods extraction only; no participant-level distribution is extracted.

comparisonClaims.claim: This run's orientation threshold is best treated as coarse psychophysical context: repeated controlled stimulus levels can estimate visual discrimination, but Boundary's short task is not a fitted laboratory threshold.
comparisonClaims.guardrail: Use this to explain why orientation discrimination can serve as a perceptual baseline; do not present the threshold as clinical, diagnostic, or directly comparable to a laboratory psychometric function.

guardrails: This extraction is methodological context, not an external participant reference distribution.
Boundary's web task uses a coarse criterion summary and may be affected by display, viewing distance, attention, and input timing.
Do not use this source to rank participants or make clinical claims.
reviewerNotes: Public-ready construct-context seed for orientation discrimination result interpretation. It supports guarded participant copy without implying cohort similarity.
